story_title: Why D J Vu Feels Familiar

clean_story:
False memory. Brain’s glitch.

Déjà vu—French for "already seen"—happens when your brain’s memory-checker misfires, mistaking the present for a stored past. It’s like a librarian handing you a book you’ve never read but swearing you checked it out last week.

Here’s the mechanism: Your hippocampus (memory hub) and rhinal cortex (familiarity detector) usually work in sync. But during déjà vu, they desynchronize. The rhinal cortex screams "I know this!" while the hippocampus whispers "Wait… do we?" This mismatch creates that eerie "I’ve been here before" feeling—even in brand-new places.

Real-world trigger: Fatigue or stress can scramble the timing. Ever walked into a room and paused because it felt like you’d done this exact thing yesterday? That’s your brain’s pattern-recognition system overcorrecting, treating random similarities (a door’s creak, a shadow’s angle) as proof of repetition.

Lab tests show déjà vu spikes in 18–25-year-olds, likely because their memory systems are still fine-tuning. It’s not precognition or past lives—just your brain’s filing cabinet dropping a folder in the wrong drawer.

Three related subjects are false memories, hippocampus malfunctions, and how stress warps time.
